title: Sweet potato soup
subtitle: Roasted sweet potato blended with harissa and cumin.
excerpt: Sweet potatoes roasted until soft and slightly caramelised, then blended with spiced vegetable stock and a spoonful of harissa. Earthy, warm, and substantial. Ready in under an hour.
sourceNotes: Sweet potato soup is British home cooking shaped by the North African and West African culinary traditions that entered the British kitchen through immigration and through cookbooks from the 1980s onwards. The harissa finish is a North African addition; the cumin-roasted method is now a British domestic standard.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze in portions for up to three months. Reheat gently and stir before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for three days in the fridge.

content.text: Sweet potato soup without roasting first can taste one-dimensional: sweet but not complex. Roasting adds caramelised edges, a deeper sweetness, and some char at the tips that gives the blended soup something to work against.

The harissa is a single spoonful in the stock, not a dominant flavour. It adds warmth and a slight smokiness. Adjust to taste: the recipe as written is mild enough for most people, including those who find spice intrusive.

items.prepNote: about 800 g total, peeled and cut into 3 cm chunks

content.text: A large roasting tin, a large saucepan, and a stick blender.

Heat the oven to 200 °C fan. Spread the sweet potato chunks and chopped onion across a large roasting tin. Add {{garlic}} of unpeeled garlic cloves. Sprinkle over {{cumin-ground}} of ground cumin and drizzle with {{olive-oil}} of olive oil. Season with {{sea-salt-flakes}} of sea salt. Toss everything together. Roast for 35–40 minutes until the sweet potato is tender and caramelised at the edges.

Squeeze the garlic from their skins and transfer everything to a large saucepan. Add {{harissa}} of harissa and pour in {{stock-vegetable}} of hot vegetable stock. Blend until smooth with a stick blender.
Warm through over a medium heat if needed. Squeeze in the lemon juice and taste for seasoning. Serve in bowls with fresh coriander and a drizzle of olive oil if you like.

items.cause: Some sweet potato varieties are significantly sweeter than others.
items.fix: Add more lemon juice and more harissa. The acid and heat balance the sweetness.

items.cause: Sweet potatoes are very high in starch and create a thick purée.
items.fix: Add more hot stock or water and blend again.

items.cause: Very sweet potatoes can overwhelm delicate spice.
items.fix: Add another teaspoon of harissa. It can handle more than the recipe suggests.

items.symptom: Pale orange colour rather than deep
items.cause: Sweet potatoes did not caramelise properly.
items.fix: For next time, ensure they are spread in a single layer. A slightly hotter oven (220 °C) for the last 10 minutes helps colour.

content.text: Sweet potato soup became part of British home cooking through the 1990s as Caribbean and West African vegetables reached mainstream supermarkets. The combination of sweet potato with North African spicing reflects the eclectic character of British domestic cookery: borrowing from multiple traditions without belonging entirely to any of them. The soup is filling enough to stand alone as lunch.
